source: Ashby App A (6th ed., 2025) + ASM Handbooks + Bath ICE v3.0

materials.name: Aluminum 2024-T3
materials.notes: Cu-Mg alloy; T3 = solution treated + cold worked. High fatigue resistance.

materials.name: Aluminum 5052-H32
materials.notes: Mg alloy; excellent corrosion resistance. H32 = strain hardened + stabilized.

materials.name: Aluminum 6061-T6
materials.notes: Most widely used structural Al alloy. Mg-Si alloy; T6 = solution treated + peak aged.

materials.name: Aluminum 7075-T6
materials.notes: Zn-Mg-Cu alloy; highest-strength common Al alloy. Lower toughness than 2024.

materials.name: Aramid/Kevlar composite (60% Vf, Kevlar 49/epoxy)
materials.notes: Fiber-direction tensile properties. Poor in compression (fibers buckle). Excellent specific strength.

materials.name: Bamboo (Moso, along grain)
materials.notes: Along-grain (longitudinal) properties. Tensile strength rivals mild steel at 1/8 the density.

materials.name: Basalt fiber composite (55% Vf, UD/epoxy)
materials.notes: Lower cost than CFRP; better temperature resistance than GFRP. Sustainable — made from volcanic rock.

materials.name: Beryllium copper C17200 (AT)
materials.notes: Highest-strength Cu alloy. AT (age tempered) condition. Beryllium dust is toxic — safety precautions required.

materials.name: Bone, cortical (human femur)
materials.notes: Properties along the longitudinal axis. Wide range reflects age/anatomical variation.

materials.name: Boron carbide (B₄C)
materials.notes: 3rd hardest material after diamond and CBN. Lightest hard ceramic.

materials.name: Brass C260 (cartridge brass, 70Cu-30Zn)
materials.notes: Most common single-phase brass. Excellent cold workability and corrosion resistance.

materials.name: Bronze C932 (SAE 660 bearing bronze)
materials.notes: Cu-Sn-Pb bronze; low friction against steel. The standard bearing/bushing bronze.

materials.name: CFRP, unidirectional (60% Vf, T300/epoxy)
materials.notes: Fiber-direction (0°) properties. T300 carbon fiber, 60% Vf. Transverse properties ~10× lower.

materials.name: Copper C110 (ETP, electrolytic tough pitch)
materials.notes: 99.9% pure Cu. Highest electrical and thermal conductivity of engineering metals.

materials.name: EPDM rubber (ethylene propylene diene)
materials.notes: Excellent UV, ozone and weather resistance. Operating range −50 to +150°C.

materials.name: Hydroxyapatite (HA), dense sintered
materials.notes: Main mineral of bone. Bioactive, osteoconductive. Brittle — not for load-bearing alone.

materials.name: Inconel 625 (nickel superalloy)
materials.notes: Ni-Cr-Mo alloy. Excellent corrosion and oxidation resistance to 980°C. Solid-solution strengthened.

materials.name: Inconel 718 (nickel superalloy)
materials.notes: Ni-Fe-Cr alloy, precipitation hardened. Workhorse of high-temperature gas turbines.

materials.name: Magnesium AZ31B (wrought)
materials.notes: Most common wrought Mg alloy. H24 condition (strain hardened + partially annealed).

materials.name: Magnesium AZ91D (die cast)
materials.notes: Most common Mg die-casting alloy. T4 treated. Very good castability.

materials.name: Polyamide 6/6 (Nylon 66, PA66)
materials.notes: Higher melting point (255–265°C) and modulus than PA6. Moisture absorbs → properties change.

materials.name: Polyimide (PI, Kapton-type)
materials.notes: No melting point (decomposes ~500°C). Continuous service to ~260°C. Excellent dielectric.

materials.name: Polysulfone (PSU)
materials.notes: Amorphous high-performance thermoplastic. Tg ~185°C; autoclave sterilisable.

materials.name: Silicon nitride (Si₃N₄, RBSN/HPSN)
materials.notes: Best combination of strength and toughness of engineering ceramics. HPSN (hot-pressed) at high end.

materials.name: Stainless steel 17-4 PH (H900)
materials.notes: Precipitation-hardened martensitic SS. H900 condition (aged at 900°F / 482°C) = peak strength.

materials.name: Stainless steel 304 (18-8)
materials.notes: Most common austenitic SS. 18% Cr, 8% Ni. Annealed condition.

materials.name: Stainless steel 316L
materials.notes: Mo-bearing austenitic SS; superior pitting/crevice corrosion resistance. Low-C grade prevents sensitization.

materials.name: Alloy steel 4140 (Q&T)
materials.notes: Cr-Mo low-alloy steel, quenched & tempered at 315°C. Good toughness-to-strength balance.

materials.name: Alloy steel 4340 (Q&T)
materials.notes: Ni-Cr-Mo steel, high hardenability. Among the highest-strength structural steels.

materials.name: Titanium Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5)
materials.notes: Most widely used titanium alloy (~50% of all Ti production). Alpha-beta alloy. Annealed condition.

materials.name: Titanium Grade 2 (commercially pure)
materials.notes: Most common CP-Ti grade. Excellent corrosion resistance. Lower strength than Ti-6Al-4V.

materials.name: Thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU)
materials.notes: Bridges gap between rubber and plastic. Excellent abrasion resistance and flex fatigue life.

materials.name: UHMWPE (ultra-high molecular weight polyethylene)
materials.notes: MW > 3.5 million g/mol. Outstanding wear and impact resistance; low friction.

materials.name: Zirconia, Y-TZP (yttria-stabilized tetragonal)
materials.notes: Transformation-toughened zirconia — highest fracture toughness of oxide ceramics (8–12 MPa√m).
